metadata.title: Build a Trademark Strength Screener with Claude
metadata.note: cli-ideas Card 15 — Ch 8 (Naming, Trademark and Brand Protection). DOUBLE-CHECK: Abercrombie spectrum 1976 Second Circuit case. Five classes: generic (never protectable), descriptive (secondary meaning required), suggestive (protectable without secondary meaning), arbitrary (strong), fanciful (strongest). 'AI Writer' = descriptive/generic, DROP. ALL legal/availability claims → [VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]. USPTO Class 9 (software) or Class 42 (SaaS).

beats.role_note: COLD OPEN LAW — Claude UI, ask shown answered; IN-FOR-BEAR
beats.narration_text: This is Liam, in for Bear. Build it with Claude, then take it apart.

props.command: claude "screen 15 name candidates for trademark strength — Abercrombie spectrum, cross-language risk, protectability"
output: 3 ADVANCE candidates shortlisted [VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]

beats.role_note: stakes — BEFORE CLI loop; SHOW-DON'T-TELL: animate the legibility-exclusivity tradeoff
beats.narration_text: Here is the counterintuitive heart of trademark law. The same property that makes a name immediately understandable — it describes what the product does — is precisely what makes it hardest to protect. And the same property that makes a name feel opaque and arbitrary at first encounter is what makes it the most defensible legal asset. A startup spent six months building a brand around a name, received a cease-and-desist, and had to start over — because the name was descriptive and a prior registration existed in the same class. The Abercrombie spectrum, from a 1976 Second Circuit case, captures this trade-off in five stops. This screener applies it to fifteen candidates before you invest.
beats.visual_intent: Animated horizontal spectrum: Generic (never protectable) → Descriptive (secondary meaning required) → Suggestive → Arbitrary → Fanciful (strongest); protectability bar rising left-to-right, legibility bar falling; example marks at each position (Software, Cold and Creamy, Netflix, Apple, Kodak); no composer yet

beats.role_note: ClaudeComposerAsk — show and discuss the prompt
beats.narration_text: Into Claude. Generate 15 name candidates for this brand, spanning the full Abercrombie spectrum. For each: spectrum class with a one-sentence rationale, memorability on a one-to-five scale, cross-language risk — does the name mean something harmful or embarrassing in Spanish, Mandarin, French, or German — dot-com availability flagged for verification, protectability score from one to five, and a recommended action: ADVANCE, SCREEN, or DROP. Tag every legal or availability assertion verify with search plus counsel. Do not claim any name is legally available.

props.command:
claude "generate 15 name candidates for:
  [BRAND DESCRIPTION + ARCHETYPE]
For each name:
  1. Spectrum: generic/descriptive/suggestive/arbitrary/fanciful
     — one-sentence rationale
  2. Memorability 1-5
  3. Cross-language risk (Spanish/Mandarin/French/German)
  4. .com availability [VERIFY]
  5. Protectability 1-5
  6. Action: ADVANCE / SCREEN / DROP
Final: top-3 ADVANCE shortlist
TAG every legal/availability claim [VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]"

beats.role_note: ClaudeCodeBeat — actual screener script; trimmed to what teaches
beats.narration_text: Read it before trusting it. The critical output is the protect-ability score and the VERIFY tag. Every line that touches legal or availability status carries the tag — not because the model is wrong about everything, but because the one thing that matters here is the one thing the model cannot actually check. The model has no access to current trademark registers. It does not run likelihood-of-confusion analysis against the live USPTO database. When it says a name is available, it is telling you it has no memory of a conflict — which means nothing at clearance.

props.title: trademark_screener.py
props.code:
# trademark_screener.py  —  Claude Code output
import anthropic, sys

SYSTEM = """You are a trademark strength screener.
For each candidate name apply the Abercrombie spectrum:
  generic | descriptive | suggestive | arbitrary | fanciful
For each output:
  spectrum_class | memorability(1-5) | cross_lang_risk |
  dot_com [VERIFY] | protectability(1-5) | action
ACTION logic:
  fanciful/arbitrary + protectability≥4 → ADVANCE
  suggestive + no cross_lang_risk      → SCREEN
  descriptive/generic                  → DROP
TAG every legal/availability claim:
  [VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]
Do NOT assert any name is legally available."""

client = anthropic.Anthropic()
result = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-opus-4-7", max_tokens=2048,
    system=SYSTEM,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": sys.stdin.read()}]
)
print(result.content[0].text)

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — 15-candidate table with spectrum classifications
beats.narration_text: Run it with AI Writer in the candidate list. Table prints: 15 names, spectrum class for each, protectability scores. AI Writer: descriptive, borderline generic, protectability 1 — DROP. The rationale prints one sentence: this term describes the product's function and is shared by the category, making exclusivity legally unavailable without years of secondary-meaning evidence. Three ADVANCE candidates at the bottom. Every legal note tagged verify with search plus counsel.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec mp4: table printing row by row — name | spectrum | protectability | action; 'AI Writer' row highlighted in red with DROP; 3 ADVANCE rows highlighted in green at bottom; '[VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]' tags visible on .com column; top-3 shortlist printed below table.

beats.narration_text: The shortlist is the starting point, not the finish line. Revise: take the top three ADVANCE candidates and run them through the archetype fit test — a Sage brand and an Outlaw brand should produce different shortlists even for the same product category. Then ask the screener to flag any name that, despite its protectability score, has a phonetic variant that is already a known mark in the software class.

props.command:
claude "filter the ADVANCE shortlist:
  archetype: [YOUR ARCHETYPE]
  For each name: does it fit the archetype register?
    (Sage names: measured/precise; Outlaw: edgy/counter)
  Flag any phonetic variants of known marks in
    software Class 9 or SaaS Class 42.
  Re-rank: top-1 recommendation.
  TAG all phonetic-conflict notes [VERIFY WITH COUNSEL]"

beats.role_note: screen-rec or animated slot — ranked shortlist with archetype fit and phonetic flags
beats.narration_text: Re-run. The three ADVANCE candidates are re-ranked by archetype fit. One is flagged with a phonetic variant note — a known mark with a similar sound in Class 9, tagged verify with counsel. The top recommendation prints with its rationale: fanciful, no cross-language risk, protectability 5, fits the Sage register. That is the candidate to take through the actual USPTO search.
beats.visual_intent: screen-rec or Manim comparison: three candidates ranked 1-2-3; candidate with phonetic flag shown with yellow warning; top candidate shown with green ADVANCE + archetype fit checkmark; '[VERIFY WITH COUNSEL]' tag on phonetic flag; clear 'TAKE THIS TO USPTO + COUNSEL' CTA at bottom.

beats.narration_text: The screener does genuine work on the generative side — forty candidates is tedious for a human and trivial for a model. What it cannot do is clear the name. Clearance requires an actual USPTO search with phonetic and visual variants, a likelihood-of-confusion analysis, and a trademark attorney. The AI+1 line in this chapter is the sharpest in the book: AI owns the generation, you own the decision, counsel owns the clearance.
beats.visual_intent: Summary card: Abercrombie spectrum visible; left column 'AI does: generate, classify, flag cross-language'; right column 'Counsel does: USPTO search, likelihood-of-confusion, clearance'; bottom: '$6 months of brand work vs. one clearance search'

beats.role_note: HANDOFF LAW — prompt READ aloud AND discussed; greeting 'Your turn.'
beats.narration_text: Your turn. Paste this prompt, add your brand description and archetype, and let the screener generate fifteen candidates. Read the protectability scores. Drop everything in the generic-to-descriptive range immediately — do not invest in a name you cannot own. Take your ADVANCE shortlist to your own USPTO search and then to counsel. The prompt does not substitute for that step. It gets you to counsel with a better shortlist and enough vocabulary to have an intelligent conversation about what you are looking for.

props.command:
claude "screen 15 trademark candidates for my brand:
  Brand: [DESCRIPTION]
  Archetype: [ARCHETYPE]
  Include 'AI [category word]' as one candidate to show
  why descriptive names fail the spectrum.
  TAG all legal/availability claims
  [VERIFY WITH SEARCH + COUNSEL]"

beats.narration_text: Build a Trademark Strength Screener with Claude. This is Liam, in for Bear — build it with Claude, then take it apart.
